content:
# Acts Chapter 10 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 237-238 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_010_page_001.jpg, ACT_010_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Now there was a certain man in Caesarea named Cornelius, a centurion of the cohort called the Italian. **2** He was devout and one who feared God with all his household, who gave many alms to the people and prayed to God continually. **3** He saw clearly in a vision, about the ninth hour of the day, an angel of God coming in to him and saying to him, "Cornelius." **4** And gazing upon him and becoming afraid, he said, "What is it, Lord?" And he said to him, "Your prayers and your alms have ascended as a memorial before God. **5** And now send men to Joppa and summon a certain Simon who is called Peter. **6** This man is lodging with a certain Simon, a tanner, whose house is by the sea."

**7** And when the angel who spoke to him had departed, he called two of his household servants and a devout soldier from among those who attended him constantly. **8** And having explained everything to them, he sent them to Joppa.

**9** And on the next day, as they were journeying and drawing near to the city, Peter went up on the housetop to pray, about the sixth hour. **10** And he became hungry and desired to eat. But while they were preparing, a trance fell upon him. **11** And he beheld heaven opened, and a certain vessel descending like a great sheet, being let down by four corners upon the earth, **12** in which were all the four-footed animals and creeping things of the earth and birds of heaven. **13** And a voice came to him, "Rise, Peter, kill and eat." **14** But Peter said, "By no means, Lord, for I have never eaten anything common or unclean." **15** And a voice came to him again a second time, "What God has cleansed, you must not call common." **16** And this happened three times, and immediately the vessel was taken up into heaven.

**17** And while Peter was greatly perplexed within himself as to what the vision that he had seen might mean, behold, the men who had been sent by Cornelius, having inquired for the house of Simon, stood at the gate. **18** And calling out, they asked whether Simon who is called Peter was lodging there. **19** And while Peter was pondering the vision, the Spirit said to him, "Behold, three men are seeking you. **20** But rise, go down, and go with them, doubting nothing, for I have sent them." **21** And Peter went down to the men and said, "Behold, I am the one whom you are seeking. What is the reason for which you have come?" **22** And they said, "Cornelius, a centurion, a righteous man and one who fears God, well spoken of by the whole nation of the Jews, was directed by a holy angel to summon you to his house and to hear words from you."

**23** So he invited them in and gave them lodging. And on the next day he arose and went out with them, and some of the brothers from Joppa accompanied him. **24** And on the following day they entered Caesarea. And Cornelius was expecting them, having called together his relatives and close friends. **25** And as it came to pass that Peter entered, Cornelius met him and fell at his feet and worshiped him. **26** But Peter raised him up, saying, "Stand up; I myself also am a man." **27** And conversing with him, he went in and found many who had come together. **28** And he said to them, "You yourselves know that it is unlawful for a Jewish man to associate with or to visit one of another nation. Yet God has shown me that I should not call any person common or unclean. **29** Therefore also I came without objection when I was sent for. So I ask, for what reason did you send for me?"

**30** And Cornelius said, "Four days ago, until this hour, I was praying at the ninth hour in my house, and behold, a man stood before me in bright clothing **31** and said, 'Cornelius, your prayer has been heard and your alms have been remembered before God. **32** Send therefore to Joppa and summon Simon who is called Peter. This man is lodging in the house of Simon, a tanner, by the sea.' **33** So I sent to you at once, and you have done well in coming. Now therefore we are all present before God to hear all the things that have been commanded you by the Lord."

**34** And Peter opened his mouth and said, "In truth I perceive that God is not one who shows partiality, **35** but in every nation the one who fears him and works righteousness is acceptable to him. **36** The word which he sent to the sons of Israel, proclaiming the good news of peace through Jesus Christ — this one is Lord of all — **37** you yourselves know the thing that happened throughout all Judea, beginning from Galilee after the baptism that John proclaimed: **38** Jesus from Nazareth, how God anointed him with the Holy Spirit and with power, who went about doing good and healing all who were oppressed by the devil, for God was with him. **39** And we are witnesses of all things that he did both in the land of the Jews and in Jerusalem — whom also they killed, hanging him on a tree. **40** This one God raised on the third day and granted that he should become manifest, **41** not to all the people, but to witnesses who had been chosen beforehand by God, to us who ate and drank with him after he rose from the dead. **42** And he commanded us to proclaim to the people and to testify solemnly that this is the one who has been appointed by God as judge of the living and the dead. **43** To him all the prophets bear witness, that everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name."

**44** While Peter was still speaking these words, the Holy Spirit fell upon all those who were hearing the word. **45** And the believers from among the circumcision, as many as had come with Peter, were astonished, because the gift of the Holy Spirit had been poured out also upon the Gentiles. **46** For they were hearing them speaking in tongues and magnifying God. Then Peter answered, **47** "Can anyone withhold the water so that these should not be baptized, who have received the Holy Spirit just as we also did?" **48** And he commanded them to be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ. Then they asked him to remain for some days.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus):**

Acts 10 begins in the first column of page 237 (folio 118v), where the final verses of chapter 9 conclude. The chapter spans all four columns of page 237 and continues across the first three columns of page 238 (folio 119r), concluding before Acts 11 begins in the fourth column. The text follows Sinaiticus's characteristic four-column majuscule format with no word division. Several correction marks by later scribes are visible throughout.

**Key Translation Decisions:**

1. **v. 2 — "devout" (eusebēs):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΥΣΕΒΗΣ. This term denotes genuine piety and religious devotion, here applied to a Gentile God-fearer, a theologically significant characterization.

2. **v. 11 — "vessel" (skeuos):** Rendered as "vessel" rather than "object" or "container" to preserve the formal register. The subsequent description ("like a great sheet") clarifies its appearance.

3. **v. 14 — "common or unclean" (koinon ē akatharton):** Both terms are preserved distinctly. "Common" (koinon) refers to what is ritually profane or shared, while "unclean" (akatharton) denotes ceremonial impurity. The distinction matters theologically as Peter's vision addresses both categories.

4. **v. 25 — "worshiped" (prosekynēsen):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΡΟΣΕΚΥΝΗΣΕΝ. This is rendered "worshiped" rather than the softer "paid homage" because the verb carries its full force here — Peter's sharp correction in v. 26 ("I myself also am a man") indicates Cornelius's action exceeded appropriate reverence.

5. **v. 30 — "Four days ago, until this hour":** This reflects the Sinaiticus reading ΑΠΟ ΤΕΤΑΡΤΗΣ ΗΜΕΡΑΣ ΜΕΧΡΙ ΤΑΥΤΗΣ ΤΗΣ ΩΡΑΣ. Some manuscripts add "I was fasting and" before "praying"; Sinaiticus includes the fasting reference (ΝΗΣΤΕΥΩΝ), which is a longer reading found in some witnesses but absent from others. Our translation follows the Sinaiticus text.

6. **v. 36 — Parenthetical "this one is Lord of all":** The Greek syntax is compressed and grammatically complex. The clause ΟΥΤΟΣ ΕΣΤΙΝ ΠΑΝΤΩΝ ΚΥΡΙΟΣ functions as a parenthetical declaration within Peter's summary of the gospel message. This is one of the most theologically dense statements in Acts, affirming Christ's universal lordship.

7. **v. 37 — "you yourselves know" (hymeis oidate):** Sinaiticus includes this address, indicating that Cornelius and his household already had some awareness of the events surrounding Jesus's ministry — consistent with their status as God-fearers connected to the Jewish community.

8. **v. 44 — "fell upon" (epepesen):** The same verb used for the Spirit's descent at Pentecost (Acts 11:15 retrospective). The dramatic, unmediated nature of the Spirit's coming — interrupting Peter's sermon — underscores the theological point that God's inclusion of Gentiles required no human intermediary beyond the proclamation of the word.

9. **v. 47 — "Can anyone withhold the water":** The rhetorical question (MĒ TI TO HYDŌR DYNATAI KŌLYSAI TIS) expects a negative answer. The word order in Sinaiticus emphasizes "the water" — the physical element — highlighting the absurdity of refusing baptism to those upon whom the Spirit has already fallen.

— LOGOS
